title: Kotlin Cheat Sheet
subTitle: Basics, Functions, Classes & OOP and more

items.title: val vs var
items.body: val is immutable (read-only) — like final in Java. var is mutable. Always prefer val; use var only when reassignment is truly needed.

items.title: Null Safety
items.body: Kotlin eliminates NPEs at compile time by distinguishing nullable (String?) from non-nullable (String).

items.title: Basic Types
items.body: Kotlin's type system unifies primitives and objects. At runtime, Int/Double etc. compile to JVM primitives where possible.
items.code:
val i: Int     = 42
val l: Long    = 42L
val d: Double  = 3.14
val f: Float   = 3.14f
val b: Boolean = true
val c: Char    = 'A'
val s: String  = "Hello"

// Type inference — compiler deduces the type
val inferred = 100        // Int
val text     = "Kotlin"   // String

// String templates
val msg = "Value is $i and double is ${i * 2}"

items.title: when Expression
items.body: when is Kotlin's switch on steroids — it's an expression, supports arbitrary conditions, and is exhaustive on sealed/enum types.

items.title: Ranges & Loops
items.body: Kotlin ranges are concise and compile to efficient index loops on the JVM.

items.title: Use data classes for models
items.body: data class auto-generates equals(), hashCode(), toString() and copy(). Perfect for API models, DTOs and UI state objects.

items.title: Avoid the !! operator
items.body: !! throws NullPointerException at runtime when the value is null. Use safe calls (?.), let blocks, or require() instead.

items.title: Equality Operators
items.body: Kotlin distinguishes structural from referential equality.

items.title: Function Syntax
items.body: Kotlin functions are first-class citizens. Single-expression functions use = instead of a block body.

items.title: Lambdas & Higher-Order Functions
items.body: Lambdas are function literals. Pass them as parameters or store them in variables.

items.title: Inline Functions
items.body: inline copies the lambda body at the call site, eliminating object allocation overhead — ideal for higher-order utility functions.

items.title: Extension Functions
items.body: Extend any class without inheritance. Resolved statically at compile time.

items.title: Scope Functions Reference
items.body: Choose the right scope function for the job.

items.title: Prefer named args over boolean flags
items.body: foo(true, false) is unreadable. Use named args: foo(active = true, visible = false). Even better — replace boolean params with an enum or dedicated data class.

items.title: Class Anatomy
items.body: Kotlin merges the constructor and property declaration into the class header.

items.title: Data Classes
items.body: data class auto-generates equals, hashCode, toString, copy and componentN functions.
items.code:
data class User(
    val id: Long,
    val name: String,
    val email: String,
)

val alice = User(1, "Alice", "alice@example.com")

// Structural equality
val alice2 = User(1, "Alice", "alice@example.com")
println(alice == alice2)  // true

// copy — change only what you need
val bob = alice.copy(id = 2, name = "Bob")

// Destructuring via componentN
val (id, name, email) = alice
println(name) // "Alice"

items.title: Sealed Classes for UI State
items.body: Sealed classes restrict the class hierarchy — perfect for exhaustive when.

items.title: Object & Companion Object
items.body: object creates a singleton. companion object holds class-level members (like static in Java).

items.title: Interfaces & Abstract Classes
items.body: Interfaces can have default implementations. Use abstract class when you need state or a constructor.

items.title: Enum Classes
items.body: Enums are type-safe constant sets. They can carry properties and implement interfaces.

items.title: Delegation
items.body: by keyword delegates interface implementation or property access to another object — eliminating boilerplate.

items.title: Prefer composition over inheritance
items.body: Kotlin classes are final by default — you must opt in with open. This nudges you toward composition. Use interface delegation (by) to share behaviour without deep hierarchies.

items.title: Collection Types
items.body: Kotlin separates read-only and mutable collection interfaces.

items.title: Transformations
items.body: map, filter, flatMap and friends are lazy-friendly and return new collections.

items.title: Aggregations
items.body: Terminal operations that fold a collection down to a single value.
items.code:
val nums = listOf(3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6)

nums.sum()                        // 31
nums.count()                      // 8
nums.count { it > 3 }             // 4
nums.min()                        // 1
nums.max()                        // 9
nums.average()                    // 3.875
nums.any { it > 8 }               // true
nums.all { it > 0 }               // true
nums.none { it < 0 }              // true
nums.first { it > 4 }             // 5
nums.firstOrNull { it > 100 }     // null

// fold — accumulate with seed
nums.fold(0) { acc, n -> acc + n } // 31

// reduce — first elem is acc
nums.reduce { acc, n -> acc + n }  // 31

// groupBy — Map<K, List<T>>
nums.groupBy { if (it % 2 == 0) "even" else "odd" }

items.title: Sorting & Ordering
items.body: Kotlin provides concise sort, sortBy, sortedWith for all common ordering needs.

items.title: Sequences — Lazy Pipelines
items.body: Sequences process elements lazily, one at a time through the chain — more efficient for large or infinite data.

items.title: Map Operations
items.body: Maps have their own rich set of transformations and access helpers.

items.title: Use Sequence for large collections
items.body: When chaining 3+ operations on 1000+ element lists, wrap with .asSequence() to avoid materialising intermediate lists. Always add a terminal operator like toList() or first() at the end.

items.title: What Are Coroutines?
items.body: Coroutines are light-weight threads managed by Kotlin. They suspend (not block) the thread, letting it do other work. Thousands can run concurrently on a handful of real threads.

items.title: launch vs async
items.body: launch is fire-and-forget. async returns Deferred<T> — await() it to get the result.

items.title: Dispatchers Reference
items.body: Pick the right dispatcher to avoid blocking the main thread.

items.title: Structured Concurrency & Scopes
items.body: Coroutine scopes ensure child coroutines are cancelled when the parent is. Never launch in GlobalScope in production.

items.title: Exception Handling
items.body: Use try/catch inside coroutines or a CoroutineExceptionHandler for unhandled exceptions.

items.title: Cancellation & Timeouts
items.body: Cancellation is cooperative — check isActive or use cancellable suspend functions.

items.title: Never block inside a coroutine
items.body: Calling Thread.sleep(), blocking I/O or synchronized {} inside a coroutine blocks the thread, killing concurrency. Use delay() instead of sleep(), and always wrap blocking calls in withContext(Dispatchers.IO).

items.title: Flow vs LiveData
items.body: Flow is Kotlin-native, cold, and composable. StateFlow/SharedFlow replace LiveData in pure-Kotlin layers. Flow is lifecycle-unaware by default — use repeatOnLifecycle or collectAsStateWithLifecycle in Android.

items.title: Creating Flows
items.body: Flows are cold — nothing runs until collected. Use flow { } builder for custom emission logic.

items.title: Flow Operators
items.body: Flows support map, filter, transform and many more intermediate operators that are lazy until collected.

items.title: StateFlow & SharedFlow
items.body: StateFlow is a hot, state-holding flow. SharedFlow is a hot broadcast channel.

items.title: Flow Error Handling
items.body: catch operator handles upstream exceptions without terminating the flow.

items.title: Flow vs StateFlow vs SharedFlow
items.body: Quick reference for choosing the right hot/cold flow type.

items.title: Generic Functions & Classes
items.body: Type parameters let you write code that works across types while remaining type-safe.

items.title: Variance: in, out, *
items.body: out (covariant) = produces T. in (contravariant) = consumes T. * = unknown projection.

items.title: Use out for read-only API return types
items.body: Declare List<out T> or Producer<out T> to make your API more flexible. Callers can assign a List<Dog> to a List<Animal> — impossible without out.

items.title: Operator Overloading
items.body: Kotlin lets you overload a fixed set of operators via named functions marked with operator.
items.code:
data class Vector(val x: Double, val y: Double) {
    operator fun plus(other: Vector)   = Vector(x + other.x, y + other.y)
    operator fun minus(other: Vector)  = Vector(x - other.x, y - other.y)
    operator fun times(scalar: Double) = Vector(x * scalar, y * scalar)
    operator fun unaryMinus()          = Vector(-x, -y)

    operator fun component1() = x
    operator fun component2() = y
}

val v1 = Vector(1.0, 2.0)
val v2 = Vector(3.0, 4.0)
val sum = v1 + v2           // Vector(4.0, 6.0)
val (x, y) = sum            // destructuring

items.title: DSL with Lambda Receivers
items.body: Lambda-with-receiver (Type.() -> Unit) enables type-safe builder DSLs.

items.title: Value Classes
items.body: Inline/value classes wrap a single value with zero runtime overhead — great for type-safe IDs.

items.title: Destructuring Declarations
items.body: Any class with componentN() operator functions supports destructuring — data classes get these for free.

items.title: Kotlin Annotations Cheatsheet
items.body: Common annotations you'll encounter in Kotlin projects.
tableRows: Expose companion member as Java static
Generate Java overloads for default-param fns
Expose property as Java field (no getter/setter)
Mark a value class for JVM inlining
Mark API as deprecated with replacement
